---
title: "Pale Tasselflower (Emilia praetermissa)"
slug: "emilia-praetermissa"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474795359/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Pale Tasselflower"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Asterales"
tax_family: "Asteraceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Asterales", "Asteraceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474795359/medium.jpeg" alt="Pale Tasselflower" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/brittanymmason">
brittanymmason</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264356064">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Asterales
-   **Family:** Asteraceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=704278)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon species in Florida found in disturbed areas such as roadsides, fields, and lawns.

## Natural history notes

A small herb characterized by its slender stems and pale pink to purplish tassel-like flower heads. In Florida, it is found in disturbed areas such as roadsides, fields, and lawns. It grows during the warmer months and is visited by a variety of small pollinators.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/704278-Emilia-praetermissa)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/genus/470)
